# What role does remote sensing play in complementing ground-based air quality monitoring for citywide pollution analysis?

# **â****Remote sensing in citywide air quality analysis**

Remote sensing revolutionizes air quality monitoring. It utilizes satellite and aerial data collection to provide a comprehensive view of urban pollution. By capturing data from afar, remote sensing technology offers a large-scale perspective of air pollution patterns. This approach helps identify pollution sources, track changes over time, and support policy development for cleaner air.

Remote sensing operates through sophisticated instruments mounted on satellites or aircraft, gathering data across vast urban areas. This enables multiple measurements simultaneously, even in regions with limited ground-based monitoring infrastructure. The collected data is then analyzed to understand distribution patterns and intensity of pollutants.  
  
**Advantages of remote sensing:**

It empowers cities with the data needed to address pollution challenges comprehensively.

## **Challenges in traditional air quality monitoring**

Traditional ground-based air quality monitoring systems face major challenges. These systems use stationary stations with [reference-grade monitors](https://www.clarity.io/blog/air-quality-monitoring-2-0-how-different-types-of-air-monitoring-technologies-are-contributing-to-a-more-holistic-understanding-of-air-pollution). Though accurate, they cover limited areas, making it difficult to fully assess urban air pollution. This problem is worse in large cities where unevenly placed monitoring stations create data gaps.

Cost is another big issue. Setting up and maintaining these stations requires significant funds for equipment, infrastructure, and skilled staff. Many regions, especially developing areas, simply cannot afford this, limiting widespread deployment.

These challenges highlight the need for additional technologies. [Remote sensing and hybrid monitoring networks](https://www.clarity.io/blog/air-quality-management-2-0-the-role-of-air-quality-monitoring-companies-and-air-pollution-measurement-technology) offer a solution by providing wider coverage and reducing reliance on ground infrastructure. By combining these technologies, cities can better understand air quality, which is crucial for effective policies.

## **How remote sensing complements ground-based systems**

Remote sensing technologies significantly enhance traditional ground-based air quality monitoring systems. By offering large-scale data collection capabilities, remote sensing fills the spatial and temporal gaps often present in conventional methods. This technology provides a broader perspective on urban air pollution, capturing data across entire metropolitan areas. It delivers crucial information on pollution distribution and intensity that stationary monitors may miss.Â

The integration of remote sensing with ground-based systemsâeven when complemented by [best practices for deploying low-cost air sensors in the urban environment](https://www.clarity.io/blog/best-practices-when-deploying-low-cost-air-sensors-in-the-urban-environment)âresults in a more comprehensive understanding of air quality dynamics.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f6bb9b58b09db262f667813/681204521027783a3bda5252_AD_4nXeio45kaM__garDM1nz-Zs45Sf2uKv6t7vL5F97eSY9n4rliDFToBrKrnmATcOioImYv-mmZhMV9UHYDTyfDQSuxgdm7Tuq_v-wNii1mI_gWot1o1nkbAmyAQDQFtWORssAtrTrDw.jpeg)

This synergy offers significant improvements in data resolution and coverage. Remote sensing collects data from multiple locations simultaneously, increasing the temporal resolution and allowing for continuous monitoring. It enables real-time updates, providing timely insights into pollution events and trends. This is especially valuable in rapidly changing urban environments where pollution levels can fluctuate quickly. By complementing ground-based data, remote sensing enhances the accuracy of air quality assessments and supports more effective policy-making.

Remote sensing is essential for modern air quality management, enabling cities to leverage cutting-edge technology for better environmental health. This integrated approach aligns with forward-thinking strategies promoted in [urban air quality management innovations](https://www.clarity.io/blog/air-quality-management-2-0-what-is-the-future-of-air-quality-management-for-large-cities-what-role-do-air-quality-sensors-play).

## **Technologies behind remote sensing**

Remote sensing technologies play a pivotal role in air quality analysis. Satellites, drones, and sensors are key components in this domain, each offering unique capabilities for monitoring pollutants.

Satellites provide an extensive overview of air quality. Equipped with advanced sensors, they orbit the Earth, capturing data on atmospheric conditions. Their ability to cover large areas makes them ideal for monitoring regional and global pollution patterns, supporting [global air quality monitoring efforts](https://www.clarity.io/blog/collaborative-efforts-in-air-quality-monitoring-a-global-perspective). Satellites gather data on various pollutants, including particulate matter and gases like nitrogen dioxide, offering insights into pollutant distribution and movement.

Drones can also be versatile tools for air quality assessment. These unmanned aerial vehicles can fly at lower altitudes, enabling them to collect detailed data in specific locations. Drones are equipped with sensors that measure pollutants directly above urban areas, industrial sites, or other points of interest. This capability allows for targeted monitoring and provides high-resolution data essential for local air quality management.

Ground-based sensors are critical for remote sensing networks. These devices are strategically placed across urban landscapes to measure pollutant concentrations at different locations. They offer real-time data, crucial for understanding air quality dynamics. [Low-cost sensors for comprehensive air quality management](https://www.clarity.io/blog/low-cost-sensors-for-comprehensive-air-quality-management) can detect a range of pollutants, providing detailed insights into air quality variations within cities.

These technologies collectively enhance the understanding of air quality, empowering cities to tackle pollution issues effectively.

## **Data integration for enhanced pollution analysis**

Data integration is crucial for urban air quality analysis. Combining remote sensing data with ground-based measurementsâas discussed in our article on [how air pollution data is helping communities breathe easier](https://www.clarity.io/blog/better-air-pollution-data-is-helping-us-all-breathe-easier-heres-how)âallows for precise identification of pollution sources and patterns. This integration provides complete data, covering both large-scale spatial information from satellites and detailed ground-level measurements. This approach improves understanding of pollutant distribution across various urban landscapes.

Effective data integration leads to better analysis. These techniques offer combined data that improves air quality assessments by bridging the spatial gaps left by traditional monitoring systems and enhancing information flow. This integration supports effective policy-making and urban planning initiatives by clearly delineating pollution patterns. Modern data integration methods align with evolving approaches in environmental intelligence, as illustrated in discussions on [collaborative air quality management approaches](https://www.clarity.io/blog/air-quality-management-2-0-how-researchers-drive-actionable-air-quality-intelligence-with-open-data-and-analysis).

Data integration lays the foundation for informed decision-making, empowering cities to tackle air pollution effectively.

## **Remote sensing's role in policy development**

Remote sensing data plays a crucial role in shaping urban air quality policies. This technology provides detailed insights into pollution hotspots and trends, enabling cities to implement effective measures for cleaner air. By integrating remote sensing data, policymakers can design targeted interventions like [low emission zones](https://www.clarity.io/blog/air-pollution-traffic-monitoring-strategies-for-reducing-emissions-in-big-cities), reducing vehicular pollution in densely populated areas. These zones restrict high-emission vehicles, improving air quality and public health.

Urban greening initiatives benefit from remote sensing data by identifying areas lacking green cover. Increasing vegetation in urban spaces helps absorb pollutants, regulate temperatures, and mitigate urban heat islands. This contributes to climate resilience and a healthier living environment. Remote sensing aids in planning these initiatives by providing accurate data on current vegetation levels and potential sites for new green spaces. Such data-driven strategies are further supported by [innovative approaches for improving urban air quality](https://www.clarity.io/blog/clean-air-cities-innovative-approaches-to-improving-air-quality-in-urban-settings) that enhance urban greening efforts.

Cities around the world have utilized remote sensing data to support low-emission zones and expand urban green spaces, demonstrating the technology's impact on policy development. This data-driven approach ensures policies are effective and responsive to the specific needs of urban environments.

## **Impacts on public health strategies**

Remote sensing data revolutionizes public health strategies by providing detailed insights into urban pollution. It identifies specific pollution sources, enabling targeted interventions to reduce exposure to harmful pollutants. This data aids in designing public health initiatives that address air quality issues effectively.

Remote sensing provides comprehensive data that informs [urban planning and public policies](https://www.clarity.io/blog/maximizing-the-impact-of-environmental-policies-through-enhanced-air-quality-monitoring). It highlights areas with high pollution levels, guiding the development of strategies to mitigate exposure. Public health agencies use this data to prioritize interventions, ensuring that resources are allocated to areas most in need.

By tracking pollution patterns, remote sensing supports the development of health advisories. [Real-time data](https://www.clarity.io/blog/how-low-cost-air-sensors-protect-public-health-during-wildfire-season) allows for immediate public alerts during high pollution events, protecting vulnerable populations. This proactive approach minimizes health risks associated with poor air quality.

Remote sensing is a crucial tool in enhancing public health strategies. It equips cities with data to implement effective measures, reducing pollution exposure and promoting healthier communities.

## **Addressing challenges in data integration**

Integrating remote sensing data with traditional air quality monitoring methods poses several challenges. These include disparities in data resolution, temporal differences, and the complexity of combining varied datasets. Remote sensing data often provides broad coverage but at a lower resolution compared to ground-based monitors. This mismatch can lead to discrepancies in data interpretation and hinder accurate analysis.

Temporal differences also complicate integration. Remote sensing data may be collected at specific intervals, while ground-based systems can offer continuous monitoring. Aligning these datasets requires sophisticated data processing techniques to ensure consistency and reliability. In many cases, advanced calibration methods play a crucial role in mitigating these discrepancies. For detailed insights on achieving standardized data quality through advanced algorithms, refer to our discussion on [how calibration helps low-cost sensors meet USEPA targets](https://www.clarity.io/blog/how-calibration-helps-low-cost-sensors-meet-usepa-and-other-performance-targets).

Data complexity further challenges integration efforts. Remote sensing involves diverse data types from satellites, drones, and ground sensors. Combining these datasets demands advanced analytical tools and expertise to process and interpret the information accurately.  
  
**Possible solutions to challenges in data integration include:**

These solutions aim to overcome integration challenges, enabling effective use of remote sensing data in urban air quality analysis.

## **Future innovations in remote sensing**

Remote sensing technology continues to evolve, promising further advancements in [air quality monitoring](https://www.clarity.io/blog/clean-air-month-takeaways-from-our-air-quality-monitoring-air-quality-101-webinar). New innovations are on the horizon, integrating artificial intelligence and wearable sensors to enhance data accuracy and accessibility.

AI integration is set to revolutionize data analysis in remote sensing. With machine learning algorithms, vast datasets from satellites, drones, and ground sensors can be processed more efficiently. AI enables the identification of complex patterns and trends in pollution data, providing deeper insights and enabling proactive measures to improve air quality.

Wearable sensors represent another frontier in remote sensing technology. These devices allow individuals to monitor their personal exposure to pollutants in real-time. By collecting localized data, wearable sensors contribute to a more detailed understanding of air quality variations in urban areas. They empower communities to take informed actions regarding their health and environment.  
  
**A recap of future innovations in remote air quality sensing:**

These innovations will expand the capabilities of remote sensing, providing more precise and actionable air quality data. As cities continue to grow, these technologies will play a vital role in maintaining and improving urban environmental health.

## **Community engagement through data transparency**

Data transparency is crucial in enhancing community engagement for air quality improvement. Providing access to remote sensing data empowers communities by offering insights into local pollution levels, as explored in our article on [how open-access air pollution data is paving the way for greater air quality awareness](https://www.clarity.io/blog/how-open-access-air-pollution-data-is-paving-the-way-for-greater-air-quality-awareness). This fosters a sense of ownership and accountability among residents, motivating collective action towards cleaner air.

Transparent data sharing builds trust between authorities and the public. It ensures that community members have the information needed to understand the environmental challenges they face. This open access to data facilitates informed discussions, leading to more effective collaboration in developing and implementing air quality initiatives.

**Engaging communities through data transparency involves several strategies:**

These approaches not only enhance community engagement but also contribute to the development of more effective and sustainable air quality policies. By fostering a collaborative environment, cities can leverage data transparency to drive meaningful change in air pollution management.

## **Key takeaways and future directions**

Remote sensing transforms urban air quality monitoring. It provides large-scale, real-time data essential for understanding pollution dynamics. This helps cities make informed decisions, enhancing public health and urban planning. Remote sensing technologies cover wide areas, offering insights that ground-based systems alone cannot achieve. They enable cities to track pollution trends, identify sources, and implement effective interventions.

As urban areas expand, remote sensing becomes more important in air quality management. It drives advancements in public health strategies and urban planning. New technologies like AI and wearable sensors enhance data accuracy and accessibility. These innovations deepen our understanding of air quality, enabling proactive measures to protect communities and the environment.

# What Challenges Face Global Air Quality Monitoring?

![A display showing earthy material, compacted soil and particulate matter underscores soil health challenges emphasizing sustainable land management and the mitigation of land degradation. The image highlights the environmental impact of unsustainable practices and industrial pollution on soil integrity. Addressing these issues requires a comprehensive approach, including soil remediation, promoting nutrient retention, and preventing further environmental damage.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/examining-soil-degradation-and-sustainable-land-management-practices-addressing.jpg "Examining Soil Degradation and Sustainable Land Management Practices Addressing Environmental Impact and Soil Pollution")

## Fundamentals

Understanding the air we breathe is paramount to our health and the health of the planet. Global air quality monitoring, therefore, stands as a **critical endeavor**, tasked with providing the necessary data to inform policies, guide public health initiatives, and empower individuals to make informed decisions. However, this seemingly straightforward mission faces a **complex web of challenges** that hinder its effectiveness and reach. These challenges span from the very basic practicalities of deploying and maintaining monitoring networks to the intricate complexities of data interpretation and utilization across diverse global contexts.

At its core, the challenge begins with **establishing a comprehensive global network**. Air pollution is not a localized issue; it’s a transboundary problem that respects no borders. To truly understand and address it, we need a monitoring system that reflects this global nature.

However, the resources, infrastructure, and political will to establish and maintain such a network are not uniformly distributed across the world. This disparity leads to significant gaps in our knowledge, particularly in regions where pollution levels are often highest and the resources to monitor are scarce.

Gaps in global air quality monitoring networks create blind spots in our understanding of pollution’s global impact, hindering effective mitigation strategies.

Consider the technical hurdles. Setting up air quality monitoring stations involves **significant upfront investment** and ongoing operational costs. Sophisticated instruments are required to measure a range of pollutants, from particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10) and ozone to nitrogen dioxide (NO2) and sulfur dioxide (SO2).

These instruments need calibration, maintenance, and secure [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  supplies → all of which can be challenging, especially in remote or resource-constrained areas. Furthermore, data transmission from these stations must be reliable and consistent, adding another layer of technological and logistical complexity.

![A mortar and pestle overflowing with dark matter represents the global challenge of pollution and environmental impact. Contrasting vibrant green elements suggest nature's persistence and potential for bio remediation. Rising dark fume underscores air pollution concerns.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/artistic-depiction-of-environmental-impact-mortar-and-pestle-symbolizing.jpg "Artistic Depiction of Environmental Impact Mortar and Pestle Symbolizing Pollution Reduction and Sustainable Practices")

### Resource Disparities

The stark reality is that wealthier nations often possess dense networks of air quality monitors, providing high-resolution data about their air. In contrast, many developing countries struggle with **sparse monitoring**, relying on limited networks that may not accurately represent the air quality experienced by their populations. This creates a global data inequality, where those most vulnerable to air pollution are often the least equipped to understand its scope and severity. Addressing this disparity is not merely a matter of technical assistance; it requires a fundamental shift towards equitable resource allocation and global cooperation.

![A seed ball, a symbol of sustainable gardening, rests in water, reflecting the delicate ecosystem. Biodegradable composition contrasts with potential water pollution suggested by floating particles. The image highlights ecological water quality impact and the importance of sustainable agriculture for environmental protection and soil conservation.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-seed-ball-resting-on-vegetation-reflecting-ecological-water-quality.jpg "Sustainable Seed Ball Resting on Vegetation Reflecting Ecological Water Quality Impact and Biodegradable Composition")

### Data Standardization and Interoperability

Beyond the physical infrastructure, another fundamental challenge lies in **data standardization**. Even when monitoring networks exist, the data they collect may not be easily comparable or interoperable across different regions or countries. Different monitoring methods, data processing techniques, and reporting standards can create a fragmented landscape of information.

To build a truly global picture of air quality, we need agreed-upon protocols for data collection, quality assurance, and sharing. This requires international collaboration and a commitment to transparency and open data principles.

Consider these points regarding the fundamentals of global air quality monitoring challenges:

![This image vividly depicts environmental pollution affecting urban infrastructure, highlighting the impact of unsustainable practices. Decayed asphalt and polluted water exemplify environmental degradation. Debris-laden water underscores the urgent need for sustainable solutions, ecological balance and pollution prevention strategies.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/visual-representation-of-environmental-pollution-showing-impact-on-sustainable.jpg "Visual Representation of Environmental Pollution Showing Impact on Sustainable Urban Infrastructure and Degradation")

## Intermediate

Moving beyond the foundational challenges, the complexities of global air quality monitoring deepen when we consider the **nuances of data interpretation and application** in diverse socio-environmental contexts. While technological advancements offer promising solutions, they also introduce their own set of intermediate-level challenges that demand careful consideration. The effectiveness of monitoring systems hinges not just on the quantity of data collected, but also on its quality, relevance, and accessibility to those who need it most.

One significant hurdle is the **calibration and validation of air quality sensors**, especially in low-cost sensor technologies that are increasingly being deployed to expand monitoring networks. While these sensors offer affordability and scalability, their accuracy and reliability can vary considerably depending on environmental conditions and sensor quality. Ensuring data accuracy across a vast network of diverse sensors requires robust calibration protocols and independent validation mechanisms. This is particularly crucial when using data from these networks for critical applications such as public health alerts or regulatory enforcement.

![A single, contaminated water droplet captured in metal represents global pollution challenges. The composition underscores environmental degradation and water scarcity, highlighting the impact of toxic runoff on natural ecosystems. Sustainable solutions, like improved water quality regulations and environmental protection, are critical to mitigate pollution and promote clean water initiatives.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/contaminated-water-droplet-encapsulated-in-metal-illustrating-environmental.jpg "Contaminated Water Droplet Encapsulated in Metal Illustrating Environmental Impact and Sustainable Solutions for Water Pollution")

### The Urban-Rural Divide

Air quality challenges often manifest differently in urban and rural environments. Cities grapple with traffic-related pollution, industrial emissions, and the urban heat island effect, leading to localized hotspots of poor air quality. Rural areas, while often perceived as cleaner, can be impacted by agricultural emissions, biomass burning, and long-range transport of pollutants from urban and industrial centers.

**Effective monitoring strategies must account for these spatial variations** and tailor monitoring approaches to the specific pollution sources and patterns in different environments. This might involve deploying mobile monitoring units in urban areas to capture hyperlocal variations or establishing specialized monitoring sites in rural areas to assess agricultural pollution.

Addressing air quality requires understanding both urban pollution hotspots and the often overlooked challenges in rural environments, demanding tailored monitoring strategies.

Furthermore, the **temporal resolution of monitoring data** is another critical factor. Many traditional monitoring stations provide hourly or even daily average concentrations. While useful for long-term trend analysis, this data may not capture short-term pollution events, such as traffic rush hour peaks or industrial emission spikes, that can have significant health impacts.

High-frequency monitoring, providing data in near real-time, is increasingly needed to inform timely public health advisories and enable individuals to take immediate actions to reduce their exposure. This requires advancements in sensor technology, data processing capabilities, and communication infrastructure.

![A sustainable green roof system integrates water management with biodiversity. Modular rectangular components capture rainwater, supporting lush green vegetation and moss, promoting a climate change resilient infrastructure. This design exemplifies a holistic approach to pollution reduction and environmental remediation by offering diverse ecosystem services.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-urban-design-integrating-green-infrastructure-for-environmental.jpg "Sustainable Urban Design Integrating Green Infrastructure for Environmental Remediation and Enhanced Ecosystem Services Impact")

### Data Accessibility and Public Engagement

The most sophisticated monitoring data is of limited value if it remains inaccessible to the public and policymakers. **Bridging the gap between data collection and actionable insights** is a major intermediate challenge. Data needs to be presented in user-friendly formats, translated into relevant information for different audiences, and disseminated effectively through accessible channels.

This includes developing public-facing air quality indices, interactive maps, and mobile applications that provide real-time air quality information and personalized health recommendations. Engaging communities in participatory monitoring initiatives can also enhance data relevance and foster a sense of ownership in air quality management.

Consider these intermediate-level challenges in global air quality monitoring:

![Erosion of concrete reveals the environmental impact of runoff water pollution, showcasing algae growth and unsustainable infrastructural decay. Layers of sediment and mineral deposits mark the pathways of water flow, which nurtures flourishing patches of algae. This illustrates a cycle of environmental degradation, connecting the urban infrastructure with watershed health and highlighting the need for environmental remediation.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/the-environmental-impact-of-runoff-algae-growth-on-eroded-concrete-surfaces.jpg "The Environmental Impact of Runoff Algae Growth on Eroded Concrete Surfaces Illustrating Water Pollution and Unsustainable Infrastructure")

## Academic

At the academic and expert level, the challenges in global air quality monitoring extend beyond technological and logistical hurdles to encompass **complex socio-political, ethical, and systemic dimensions**. These challenges demand a critical and interdisciplinary approach, recognizing air pollution as not just an environmental problem, but a deeply entrenched issue intertwined with economic development models, global power dynamics, and social justice concerns. Moving towards effective global air quality [governance Meaning → Governance, in an ESG context, refers to the systems of rules, practices, and processes by which a company is directed and controlled.](https://esg.sustainability-directory.com/)  requires a fundamental rethinking of monitoring paradigms and data utilization strategies.

One critical academic challenge lies in **integrating air quality monitoring data with broader [sustainability Meaning → Sustainability is the capacity for human civilization to coexist with the Earth's biosphere in a state of equilibrium.](https://sustainability-directory.com/)  frameworks**. Air pollution is intrinsically linked to climate change, resource depletion, and ecosystem degradation. Understanding these interconnections is crucial for developing holistic solutions. For instance, policies aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions often have co-benefits for air quality, and vice versa.

Academic research needs to focus on developing integrated assessment models that can quantify these co-benefits and trade-offs, informing policy decisions that address air pollution within a wider sustainability context. This also necessitates moving beyond single-pollutant monitoring to multi-pollutant approaches that capture the complex mixture of pollutants and their synergistic effects on health and the environment.

![This spherical planetoid, with mossy rock, features diverse spheres and delicate strands that evokes the challenges of environmental degradation. The sphere's elements depict a planet under climate change, suggesting a need for ecosystem restoration, environmental stewardship, and strategies for environmental sustainability. The image highlights environmental pollution, biodiversity loss, emphasizing carbon neutrality and climate action as crucial measures for a more sustainable future.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/a-textured-globe-with-moss-and-spheres-evokes-sustainable-resilience-in-the.jpg "A Textured Globe with Moss and Spheres Evokes Sustainable Resilience in the Face of Environmental Degradation")

### Geopolitical and Governance Complexities

Air pollution is a transboundary issue, yet global governance mechanisms for air quality remain fragmented and weak. **Geopolitical tensions, varying national priorities, and lack of international legal frameworks** hinder effective cross-border cooperation on air pollution monitoring and control. Academic research can play a critical role in analyzing these governance gaps and proposing innovative institutional arrangements for global air quality management.

This includes exploring mechanisms for international data sharing, technology transfer, and financial assistance to support developing countries in strengthening their monitoring capacities and implementing pollution control measures. The challenge also involves navigating the complexities of national sovereignty and building trust and consensus among diverse stakeholders.

Global air quality governance is hampered by geopolitical complexities and weak international frameworks, demanding innovative solutions for cross-border cooperation and data sharing.

Furthermore, the academic community needs to critically examine the **ethical dimensions of air quality monitoring**, particularly in the context of data ownership, privacy, and potential misuse of information. As monitoring becomes more pervasive and data-driven, questions arise about who owns air quality data, how it is used, and who benefits from it. There is a risk that monitoring data could be used for surveillance or discriminatory purposes, exacerbating existing social inequalities.

Ethical frameworks and guidelines are needed to ensure that air quality monitoring is conducted in a responsible and equitable manner, respecting individual privacy and promoting public good. This includes addressing issues of data bias and ensuring that monitoring efforts are focused on protecting vulnerable populations and addressing environmental injustices.

![Smoke rises from an artificial green surface, a potent visual metaphor for pollution and the environmental impact of unsustainable practices. Droplets of moisture dot the scene, hinting at water contamination. This image challenges viewers to consider the complexities of sustainable development, ecological footprint, and the crucial need for environmental responsibility.](https://pollution.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/visualizing-environmental-impact-smoke-emanating-from-artificial-green-surface.jpg "Visualizing Environmental Impact Smoke Emanating from Artificial Green Surface Highlighting Pollution and Sustainability Challenges")

### Challenging Dominant Development Paradigms

Perhaps the most profound academic challenge lies in **questioning the dominant development paradigms** that perpetuate air pollution. Many current economic models prioritize economic growth at the expense of environmental and public health. Addressing air pollution effectively requires a fundamental shift towards more sustainable development pathways that decouple economic progress from environmental degradation.

Academic research can contribute to this shift by exploring alternative economic models, promoting circular economy principles, and advocating for policies that prioritize [well-being Meaning → Well-being encompasses holistic health and happiness, supported by sustainable lifestyle choices that benefit both individuals and the environment.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/)  and environmental sustainability over purely economic metrics. This also involves challenging consumerism, promoting sustainable lifestyles, and fostering a deeper societal understanding of the true costs of pollution and the benefits of clean air.

Consider these academic-level challenges in global air quality monitoring:

| Challenge Area | Description | Academic Focus |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Sustainability Integration** | Disconnect between air quality monitoring and broader environmental frameworks. | Developing integrated models to assess co-benefits and trade-offs with climate and other sustainability goals. |
| **Geopolitical Governance** | Fragmented international cooperation and weak global governance mechanisms. | Analyzing governance gaps and proposing innovative institutional arrangements for global air quality management. |
| **Ethical Considerations** | Data ownership, privacy, and potential misuse of monitoring information. | Developing ethical frameworks for responsible and equitable air quality monitoring and data utilization. |
| **Development Paradigms** | Dominant economic models prioritize growth over environmental health, perpetuating pollution. | Exploring alternative economic models and sustainable development pathways that decouple growth from pollution. |

SUMMARY:

FULL CONTENT:
DRAFT – This is a draft report section under consideration by the AQM Subcommittee. Air Quality Management SubCommittee Michael Bradley & John Bachman Challenges for Air Quality Management – A Look Ahead Over the past thirty years, air quality management programs in the United States have made significant progress in a number of key areas. For instance, the number of areas out of attainment with air quality standards has declined dramatically; air quality standard violations of several pollutants, including sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxide, carbon monoxide and lead, have been nearly eliminated; and the concentrations of the other criteria pollutants have dropped considerably in much of the country. This progress has resulted in substantial public health benefits and economic savings, during a period of sustained growth in the economy, energy production, vehicular travel, and population (Figure 1). Yet a number of serious air quality management challenges remain, from the areas with lingering nonattainment problems with ozone and particulate matter to heightened awareness and concern over exposure to air toxics; from the relatively high background levels of air pollution (some of it from international transport) to the effect of air pollution on climate change – and vice versa. In addition, the economic and societal factors that influence air pollution to continue to grow. To be effective, future air quality management will need to address all of these challenges. Continued Nonattainment Problems: Ozone and Particulate Matter Despite the implementation of the federal NOx SIP Call, the Clean Air Interstate Rule (CAIR), federal mobile source rules, and various state, local, and tribal initiatives, our air quality modeling forecasts suggest that a number of areas would remain out of attainment with the current national ambient air quality standards for ozone and particulate matter in 2015 even after such programs are implemented (Figure 2). The number of residual non-attainment areas would be increased if the proposed revisions to the PM2.5 standards are promulgated. This attainment ‘gap’ must be addressed by the AQM system between now and 2020. CAIR, which aims to cut NOx and PM emissions from electric generating sources by around 60 percent by 2015, along with tighter federal mobile source rules, are still predicted to leave 14 areas in nonattainment with either PM2.5 or ozone standards in 2015. These nonattainment areas, according to EPA modeling, are expected to be concentrated in California and in a geographic region between Michigan and Alabama, including Atlanta. The common thread in eastern projected nonattainment areas appears to be higher regional PM2.5 and ozone levels. For PM, this regional problem is expected to be exacerbated by concentrations of local sources of direct PM emissions such as industrial facilities. These lingering nonattainment areas are of particular concern given the increased scientific evidence which has emerged over the past decade linking ozone and particulate DRAFT – This is a draft report section under consideration by the AQM Subcommittee. matter exposure to a wide range of serious human health effects. In addition to the long-recognized effects of ozone on lung function, more recent scientific studies have linked ozone to mortality (particularly among the elderly), hospital admissions for respiratory ailments (particularly among children), school absenteeism, and incidence of asthma. Likewise, scientists now better understand the very serious health effects associated with fine particulate matter exposure. Numerous studies had previously linked PM to a wide range of cardiovascular and respiratory health problems; new studies demonstrate associations between short-term exposure and various indicators of PM and cardiopulmonary mortality, hospitalization and emergency department visits, respiratory symptoms, and the development of lung capacity in children. The evidence now shows an association with cardiovascular health problems, including increased heart attacks, development of atherosclerosis, and changes in blood chemistry. Children and the elderly, as well as people with pre-existing cardiovascular or respiratory diseases such as asthma, are particularly susceptible to health effects caused by PM. One of the difficulties in addressing particulate pollution is the wide range of sources that produce direct and/or secondarily formed PM, from diesel engines in on-road and off-road vehicles, to electric generating facilities, industrial combustion and process sources, and residential woodstove use. Moreover, in some areas, a substantial fraction of particulate matter pollution emanates upwind and contributes to local problems. These background levels of pollution are extremely hard for state, local, and tribal air quality planners to address, yet they must be considered. Another challenge for air quality management that has surfaced in recent years involves the speciation of particulate matter; that is, the various types of particles (e.g., sulfates, nitrates, carbon, and crustal). A number of key questions remain: which types of particles or source types are most toxic? Which contribute to the most serious public health, climate, and ecosystem effects? The answers to these questions may assist air quality managers hone strategies to address the greatest threats among particulates. As the NRC report recognized, an emerging area of concern for both ozone and particulate matter is the growing evidence that there is no clear threshold, or level below which no serious health impacts will occur. Studies of both pollutants suggest that there may be no threshold, and even low level exposures to ozone or PM may be harmful to human health. The most recent scientific information on the health and environmental effects of particles, ozone, and related precursors suggests that the standards and programs for these pollutants will likely remain at current or even more restrictive levels for the foreseeable future. Developing strategies to attain and maintain current or tighter standards in the long-term will pose a significant challenge for the air quality management system. It appears likely that this will require new and innovative emissions reductions strategies; drawing in under-regulated emissions sources, such as marine vessels, locomotives, and grandfathered industrial facilities; instituting transportation control measures to address DRAFT – This is a draft report section under consideration by the AQM Subcommittee. increasing vehicle use; and initiating regional planning efforts to engage in a more holistic approach to air quality management. Air Toxics Historically, the air quality management system has not allocated the same level of resources to air toxics control and management efforts as compared to ozone and PM. While ozone and PM programs have resulted in the reduction of a number of toxic components and precursors, residual air toxics problems still exist on local, regional, and even global scales, and the NRC report and recent National Air Toxics Assessment (NATA) work suggest that exposure to various air toxics pose significant risks to public health. The most recent NATA report suggests a background cancer risk for much of the nation in the range of between 1 and 25 in a million, with much of that coming from a single compound, benzene. Moreover, the areas of higher risk tend to occur in populated urban areas and in the eastern U.S., which also tend to overlap the ozone and PM non-attainment areas. It is useful to separate air toxics into two categories: 1) persistent bioaccumulative toxics such as mercury and other heavy metals, dioxins, and pesticides. Such toxics often have a long atmospheric lifetime and are prone to long distance transport (hundreds to thousands of miles) and multimedia pathways to human exposure, often through ingestion of contaminated foods that have concentrated substances deposited from atmospheric transport. To a large extent, dealing with these toxics is a matter of addressing the sources – including those located abroad – that contribute to the buildup of background concentrations. 2) high-risk species with short-to-medium term atmospheric lifetimes. These ‘traditional’ air toxics have more of a local impact through direct inhalation and are much more likely than bioaccumulative pollutants to pose environmental justice concerns. These pollutants, particularly those from stationary sources from industrial to area in size, have been the subject of Section 112 regulations. Petroleum refining, mineral extraction and smelting operations, hazardous waste combustion, and various other source categories have all been the subject of Section 112 “Maximum Achievable Control Technology” regulations. In many urban areas, “traditional” air toxics exposures are dominated by mobile source emissions. Toxic “hot spots” often occur in predominantly low-income communities situated adjacent to major highways, congested roads, transit depots, marine and rail terminals, as well as near commercial and industrial sources. Emerging information points to a potential overlap between traditional air toxics and PM concerns. A growing body of evidence suggests both exposures and health effects of concern for populations who spend significant time on or near heavily-traveled roadways. The issue may be related to direct localized emissions of ultrafine, fine, or even coarse particles, associated organic or inorganic gaseous tailpipe emissions, or multiple factors, including toxic subcomponents of such emissions. While this area is the subject of increasing research activity, it is important to note that National Ambient Air Quality Standards and State Implementation Plans have not developed effective strategies to deal DRAFT – This is a draft report section under consideration by the AQM Subcommittee. with micro-environments such as these. Instead, ‘hot-spots’ would require different and innovative management techniques that could include transportation planning, city planning, and a variety of mitigating actions (e.g., diesel retrofits). Other Effects of Air Pollution/Interactions In addition to addressing the lingering nonattainment problems for ozone and PM and ongoing air toxics problems, air quality management must also confront other effects of air pollution including climate change and ecosystem impacts. Climate Change Warmer temperatures and air pollution experience a dynamic relationship, as each may exacerbate or mitigate the other. For instance, rising temperatures cause greater ozone production, so a warming earth may lead to more ozone pollution in many areas. In addition, warmer weather directly would affect energy demand: as temperatures rise, so too does electricity use. More electricity use would lead to greater utilization of existing power plants, or (eventually) to more power plants. In turn, this would lead to more NOx, SOx, PM, VOC and CO emissions in the summer. A warmer winter may also lead to less energy demand, which would help in areas where PM2.5 problems are dominated by woodsmoke and related wintertime heating emissions. Another possible—though not certain—impact of global warming is an increase in the frequency of wildfires, generally because of hotter and drier conditions or because of other consequences of climate change (for instance, climate change may cause greater seasonal variations in rainfall in certain locations. More rain may lead to more vegetative growth; if hotter and drier conditions prevail as the season progresses, the trees and plants may become a tinderbox and cause more extensive wildfires because of their greater density.) An increase in wildfires will have a direct impact on air quality, as fires emit various air pollutants, such as PM2.5, SO2, NOx, and a host of hazardous air pollutants including benzene, toluene, and polycyclic organic matter. Air quality linkages to climate work in the other direction as well, because some air pollutants may affect global warming For instance, while carbon dioxide and tropospheric ozone help to warm the globe, sulfates resulting from sulfur dioxide emissions serve to cool it. A number of scientists believe that the relatively cool period at the beginning of the second half of the twentieth century was tied to an increase in the emission of sulfates. More recently, scientists have begun to focus on the regional and local scale effects of air pollutants on weather and climate, and here the effects may go beyond the traditional focus on simple warming and cooling by aerosols and gases. One of the key points is illustrated by recent global simulation modeling done by Mark Jacobson of Stanford University. He reports two important findings. First, reducing particulate matter concentrations in the Eastern U.S. may produce warming, since sulfates cause cooling by DRAFT – This is a draft report section under consideration by the AQM Subcommittee. increasing cloud cover that reduces sunlight reaching the ground and increases that reflected into space. Second, Jacobson builds into his model findings that the increased cloudiness associated with the sulfate particles comes with a decrease in cloud droplet size, which results in reduced precipitation. This illustrates an important potential regional effect of air pollution, namely that air pollution can affect climate on a regional scale, and some of the effects – reduced precipitation – may be problematic. Recent results from researchers at the Desert Research Institute suggest that atmospheric sulfates may be reducing the amount of snow pack accumulation in the Rocky Mountains, potentially aggravating drought conditions. These results illustrate the importance of examining the unexpected feedbacks between air pollution and climate. The bottom line is that understanding the connections between global warming and air quality management is not easy, and there is great uncertainty, particularly over the timing, extent, scale and localized impact of these potential effects. Nonetheless, the effect of climate change on air quality and vice versa is far too important a concern to ignore. For air quality managers, these multifaceted linkages could include the following: 1) it is vital to develop a system that attempts to anticipate the potential impacts of forecast climate changes on air quality 2) The AQM system must anticipate and provide for the possible need for conventional and innovative multipollutant programs that address both conventional air pollutants as well as greenhouse gases; 3) Future programs may also need to address the effects of air pollution on regional or local climate in the US. Environmental Effects Historically, Federal and State air quality management programs have not focused significant attention on environmental effects of air pollution, including effects on terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems or visibility, as compared to health based programs. As evidence on some of these effects mounted in the 1970s and 1980’s Congress amended the Clean Air Act to address visibility in national parks and wilderness areas (Section 169A and B, 1977 and 1990 Amendments) and acid rain (Title IV,1990 Amendments). Both are very significant programs that effected a substantial decline in emissions of SOx and NOx that began with the acid rain program and is expected to continue over the next several decades through the regional haze rule. Nevertheless, the AQM system has not produced much beyond these explicitly mandated environmental programs. Yet, the Act also launched programs to research ecosystem health and it still retains requirements for secondary standards to protect public welfare, including the environment. The NRC AQM report recommended establishing ecosystems as an air quality management priority. It suggested that this would entail, as a first step, implementing a monitoring system to measure ecosystem health. An adequate measurement system would involve not only increasing the current number of measurement locations, but also developing meteorological and exposure models, undertaking risk assessment research and researching the interplay of ecosystems with multiple factors simultaneously, such as air quality, climate, and topography.

Abstract
--------

The use of low-cost environmental sensors has gained significant attention due to their affordability and potential to intensify environmental monitoring networks. These sensors enable real-time monitoring of various environmental parameters, which can help identify pollution hotspots and inform targeted mitigation strategies. Low-cost sensors also facilitate citizen science projects, providing more localized and granular data, and making environmental monitoring more accessible to communities. However, the accuracy and reliability of data generated by these sensors can be a concern, particularly without proper calibration. Calibration is challenging for low-cost sensors due to the variability in sensing materials, transducer designs, and environmental conditions. Therefore, standardized calibration protocols are necessary to ensure the accuracy and reliability of low-cost sensor data. This review article addresses four critical questions related to the calibration and accuracy of low-cost sensors. Firstly, it discusses why low-cost sensors are increasingly being used as an alternative to high-cost sensors. In addition, it discusses self-calibration techniques and how they outperform traditional techniques. Secondly, the review highlights the importance of selectivity and sensitivity of low-cost sensors in generating accurate data. Thirdly, it examines the impact of calibration functions on improved accuracies. Lastly, the review discusses various approaches that can be adopted to improve the accuracy of low-cost sensors, such as incorporating advanced data analysis techniques and enhancing the sensing material and transducer design. The use of reference-grade sensors for calibration and validation can also help improve the accuracy and reliability of low-cost sensor data. In conclusion, low-cost environmental sensors have the potential to revolutionize environmental monitoring, particularly in areas where traditional monitoring methods are not feasible. However, the accuracy and reliability of data generated by these sensors are critical for their successful implementation. Therefore, standardized calibration protocols and innovative approaches to enhance the sensing material and transducer design are necessary to ensure the accuracy and reliability of low-cost sensor data.

**Keywords:** low-cost sensors, water quality, air quality, calibrations

1. Introduction
---------------

Climate change is a significant challenge for environmental sustainability [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B1-sensors-24-03650),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B2-sensors-24-03650)], and to address this issue effectively, it is crucial to advance scientific knowledge by collecting and comprehending information on various aspects related to climate change [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B3-sensors-24-03650)]. This requires a comprehensive understanding of the global environmental system and data collection is the most crucial part of this process.

The environment is facing significant challenges worldwide, particularly in terms of pollution which substantially degrades it. These challenges are largely due to a combination of factors, including population growth, the ageing of infrastructure, the impacts of climate change, and ongoing global development [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B4-sensors-24-03650),[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B5-sensors-24-03650)]. Environmental pollution including water, air and soil pollution has become prevalent globally. More research needs to be conducted to effectively monitor and understand the sources, concentrations, and effects of environmental pollutants to aid policymakers and citizens in developing strategies for preventing pollution and protecting the environment, particularly in vulnerable regions.

Thus, there is an urgent need to develop a more effective and rapid method of monitoring environmental pollution given that the traditional methods of collecting data and samples which often involve laboratory analysis are expensive, time-consuming, and labour-intensive. Also, traditional data collection methods are not real-time and lack the fast data collection and dissemination which are needed for an effective and timely response to protect the environment [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B6-sensors-24-03650),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B7-sensors-24-03650)]. The traditional method of monitoring the environment, e.g., water and air quality parameters, involves manual collection of samples from different areas and carrying out several laboratory analyses to process, analyse and characterise samples. These traditional methods for environmental monitoring are now seen as inadequate for effectively monitoring the environment, partly because they are also prone to human error [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B6-sensors-24-03650),[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B8-sensors-24-03650)]. This has led to several researchers emphasising the need for developing low-cost, robust, and standard methods and sensors for identifying and quantifying pollutants in the environment.

Environmental attributes such as the quality of water, air, and soil are usually observed through traditional sensors located at established monitoring stations. These conventional in-situ methods, employing stationary sensors, come with limitations in data resolution and necessitate intensive training and maintenance. Meanwhile, when using satellite data, challenges arise due to disparities in spatial and temporal scales compared to environmental occurrences [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B9-sensors-24-03650)]. Furthermore, the need for increased data collection density has surged over the last two decades, driven by population growth and escalating levels of air and water pollution.

Recent advancements in digital electronics, wireless communication technologies, and sensor manufacturing [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B10-sensors-24-03650)] have generated a growing demand within the field of environmental science for low-cost sensor networks (LCSNs). These networks are increasingly valuable for addressing both fundamental research inquiries and practical management challenges [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B11-sensors-24-03650),[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B12-sensors-24-03650)]. This shift in approach is driven by the accessibility of low-cost sensors (LCSs) equipped with user-friendly technologies and calibration methods that yield data with enhanced spatial resolution [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B13-sensors-24-03650),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B14-sensors-24-03650),[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B15-sensors-24-03650)]. Several factors, including the decreased costs of microcontrollers for sensors, environmental sensor components, and straightforward communication modules, have played pivotal roles in bringing about this shift. Additionally, the expanded spatial coverage afforded by LCSs enables the generation of fresh insights into environmental dynamics [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B16-sensors-24-03650)].

The term ‘low-cost’ sensor does not define a specific price range, as the cost can vary depending on the specific parameters being measured. It can be defined as a sensor that is relatively inexpensive to produce, purchase, and maintain compared to other sensors with similar functionalities [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B14-sensors-24-03650)].

Low-cost sensors are the latest and most innovative technology used in monitoring water and air quality in real time. The use of low-cost sensors for environmental sensing and monitoring is increasing due to the availability and affordability of low-cost sensors, internet facilities, and cloud computing services [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B8-sensors-24-03650),[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B17-sensors-24-03650)]. Low-cost sensors also require fewer human interventions to operate and thus are less biased compared to traditional techniques and can be deployed in remote and inaccessible locations.

Wireless network sensors, for example, have become popularly employed by researchers for environmental monitoring of factors such as water quality parameters e.g., temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, turbidity, water flow rate, and conductivity [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B18-sensors-24-03650),[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B19-sensors-24-03650)]. They have also been used to measure air quality parameters such as particulate matter, carbon monoxide, and nitrogen dioxide [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B20-sensors-24-03650),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B21-sensors-24-03650)]. For example, in the past 10 years, studies such as [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B6-sensors-24-03650),[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B22-sensors-24-03650)] have attached water and air quality sensors, respectively, to Arduino controllers; an open-source, user-friendly, and simple platform to measure and monitor water quality parameters including dissolved oxygen, pH, temperature, nitrates, and turbidity in their studies.

The real-time water quality data from the above studies were acquired, processed, and automatically transmitted through Internet of Things (IoT) systems. A network of low-cost sensors that can collect real-time data will aid in the detection and understanding of the sources and pathways of pollution in the environment large and remote. This will be significant in effectively modelling and monitoring the vulnerability of human health and the ecosystem to environmental pollution.

However, any sensor that is more affordable than the instrumentation needed to meet regulatory requirements for the parameter under study is categorized as low-cost [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B22-sensors-24-03650)]. In this context, the cost of sensors typically increases when additional components such as microprocessors, data loggers, memory cards, batteries, and display units are incorporated.

The increased adoption of low-cost sensors (LCSs) in recent studies can be attributed to the user-friendly nature of these sensors, which allows for a cost-effective expansion of spatial coverage that has been traditionally limited. While the existing literature acknowledges the value of LCSs as a valuable addition to the commonly used measurement tools, it consistently highlights the potential for sensor misuse leading to more frequent inaccuracies. It is important to note that data collected from these sensors are indicative of specific locations and their ambient conditions, suggesting underlying factors affecting sensor measurements. In essence, the selectivity of a sensor refers to its ability to differentiate between the intended target and any interfering elements [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B23-sensors-24-03650)]. For example, a gas sensor designed to detect one type of particle often exhibits sensitivity to other particles, which can interfere with the accurate measurement of the target pollutant or particle. This phenomenon is known as sensor cross-sensitivity and can be assessed by exposing the sensor to other pollutants [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B24-sensors-24-03650)].

A standard reference sensor tends to show a higher sensitivity to particles, therefore is more precise, and more selective to measure a specific variable of interest. Therefore, according to WMO reports, low-cost sensors should be used under established quality assurance and quality control protocols [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B25-sensors-24-03650),[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B26-sensors-24-03650),[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B27-sensors-24-03650)]. Further, a more precise calibration approach will be attained with selectivity and sensitivity of a low-cost sensor at any location. Therefore, to use an LCS instrument, a published standard set of criteria must be followed which should be provided by regulatory agencies. Selectivity and sensitivity are two essential factors to take into account while considering an LCS for any given investigation.

Low-cost sensors with higher spatial resolution can provide us with better regional accuracy and offer us even more liberty to choose the variables that are appropriate for the region. The existing literature demonstrates, however, that low-cost sensors suffer from significant uncertainties because of large data outliers, weak correlations, and low data precision [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B28-sensors-24-03650),[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B29-sensors-24-03650)]. The selectivity of the sensors may improve the evaluation, but more thorough calibration procedures that address the difficulties that have been raised can produce improved results.

Sensor calibration is the process of comparing the output of the instrument or sensor under test against the output of an instrument of known accuracy when the same input is applied to both instruments [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B30-sensors-24-03650)] by developing a mathematical function that describes the relationship between the uncalibrated variables and the reference [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B28-sensors-24-03650)]. However, the relationship between uncalibrated and reference is not a direct proportion; there exists the influence of multiple other parameters. Therefore, the calibration function can be improved by utilising cross-sensitive parameters that influence the parameter of interest. Automatic and semi-automatic calibration methods are two calibration methods that are largely used for LCSs [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B13-sensors-24-03650),[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B28-sensors-24-03650),[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B31-sensors-24-03650),[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B32-sensors-24-03650)]. This review article aims to answer several questions related to the increasing popularity of low-cost sensors as an alternative to high-cost sensors. The article explores why selectivity and sensitivity of sensors are crucial factors in low-cost sensors, and how calibration functions can improve accuracy. Additionally, the article discusses ways to enhance the accuracy of sensors, providing insights into the development of low-cost sensing technologies.

2. Literature Survey Approach
-----------------------------

The literature survey is conducted in a structured approach which covers identification, screening, eligibility assessment, and final inclusion. The PRISMA Statement, which outlines the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-Analyses, was the principal criterion used in this study. PRISMA can be defined as guidelines that provide a structured framework for authors to follow when writing and reporting systematic reviews and meta-analyses [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B33-sensors-24-03650)]. Its use by Cochrane Collaboration defines the systematic review as “an examination of a clearly formulated questions that uses systematics and explicit methods to identify, select, and critically appraise relevant research and to collect and analyse data from the studies that are included in the review. Statistical methods may or may not be used to analyse and summarise the results of the included studies” [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B34-sensors-24-03650),[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B35-sensors-24-03650)]. The primary search criteria applied for identification of the articles in each database are as follows: “(“water quality” or “air quality” AND ‘low-cost sensors’ AND “calibration”)” and with time-period 2013 to 2022. The number of articles identified from the databases SCOPUS, Science Direct, and Web of Science are 127, 12, and 1, respectively. The returned articles were uploaded to the Rayyan online platform [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#B36-sensors-24-03650)] for screening and preselection of publications for further review of articles. We excluded papers that were not in English, unpublished, or duplicates, resulting in 70 papers being selected for further review. In the subsequent phase, eight of the authors independently evaluated the title, abstract, and conclusions of these 70 papers based on specific inclusion and exclusion criteria detailed in [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#sensors-24-03650-t001) to determine their relevance. This process identified 46 relevant papers and a thorough full-text review of the 46 shortlisted papers was conducted. To write this study, we looked at more than just the aforementioned papers and book chapters to determine the types of sensors for each relevant parameter since there are numerous sensors made explicitly for monitoring specific parameters. The PRISMA diagram as applicable to this systematic review study is shown in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11175279/#sensors-24-03650-f001).
